sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=24, col=32).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 54, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blob stayed at 65 pixels after 5 moves - the square moved through already-cleared space, not new blob cells. Square is now at (24-25, 30-31) at the blob's top-left edge. Need to move the square through UNTOUCHED blob cells. Let me try moving right through the blob's main body.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 34 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Blob still at 65 pixels! The square moved from (24-25, 30-31) to (23-24, 31-32) but the blob didn't shrink. This means the square moved into cells that were NOT part of the blob. Looking at the blob boundary, (23, 31) is on the boundary, so the cells around it might be outside the blob.
Wait, I think I've been misunderstanding the mechanic. Let me reconsider.
When the square first entered the blob (from outside), the blob lost pixels. But when the square moves within the blob's former territory, the blob doesn't lose more pixels because those cells are no longer part of the blob.
Actually, I think the blob is a fixed shape. When the square moves into blob cells, those cells become M (magenta) and are no longer counted as blob cells. But the blob doesn't "regenerate" - it just loses those cells permanently.
So the question is: where are the remaining 65 blob pixels? They must be in cells that the square hasn't touched yet.
Let me look at the blob boundary again: [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29], [26, 30], [26, 31], [25, 32], [24, 32]]
This traces an irregular shape. The interior of this boundary contains the blob cells. The square has been moving through the top-left portion of this shape, consuming cells. But the blob still has 65 pixels, meaning most of the blob is still intact.
Wait, but the blob started at 69 pixels and only lost 4 pixels (69→65). That means only 4 cells have been consumed so far. The square is 4 cells, so it consumed exactly one "square's worth" of blob cells.
Hmm, but I moved the square multiple times through the blob. Let me recount:
1. First entry: square at (31-32, 37-38) → blob boundary included (31, 37). After move: blob 68 pixels. 1 cell consumed.
2. Second entry: square at (30-31, 36-37) → blob boundary changed. After move: blob 65 pixels. 3 cells consumed.
3-7. Five more moves: blob stayed at 65 pixels. 0 cells consumed.
8. One more move right: blob stayed at 65 pixels. 0 cells consumed.
So only 4 cells were consumed total (1 + 3 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 4). The square is 4 cells, so it consumed exactly one square's worth of blob cells when it first entered.
This means: the blob only loses cells when the square moves from OUTSIDE the blob to INSIDE the blob. Once the square is inside, moving it around doesn't consume more blob cells because the square is just moving through space that's already been "claimed" by the square.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me think again.
Actually, I think what's happening is:
- The blob is a connected region of blue cells.
- When the square moves, its 4 cells overwrite whatever was there.
- If the square's new cells overlap with blob cells, those blob cells become M and the blob loses those pixels.
- But the square's OLD cells become background (B), not blob. So if the square was inside the blob and moves, the blob loses the new overlapping cells but the old cells don't become blob again.
So the net change per move is: (new blob cells overwritten) - 0 (old cells don't regenerate blob).
In my first move into the blob: square at (31-32, 37-38), the cell (31, 37) was a blob cell. After moving to (30-31, 36-37), the new cells (30, 36), (30, 37), (31, 36) were blob cells, and (31, 37) was already M. So 3 new blob cells were overwritten. Blob: 69 → 68 (1 cell) → 65 (3 cells).
Wait, that's 4 cells total consumed, which matches. But then in the next 5 moves, the square moved through cells that were already M (from the square's previous positions) or B (background), not blob cells. So no more blob cells were consumed.
This means the blob has a specific shape, and the square's path only passed through 4 blob cells total. The remaining 65 blob cells are in a different part of the blob that the square hasn't reached.
So the strategy should be: move the square through the blob in a way that maximizes the number of blob cells it passes through. This means moving the square in a straight line through the blob's longest dimension, or moving it in a zigzag pattern to cover more area.
But wait - each move only overwrites 3 NEW cells (since 1 cell overlaps with the previous position). So to consume 65 blob cells, I'd need about 65/3 ≈ 22 moves, assuming each move passes through 3 blob cells.
But my recent moves didn't consume any blob cells because the square was moving through already-cleared space. I need to find a path through the blob that passes through UNTOUCHED blob cells.
Let me look at the blob boundary more carefully to figure out where the untouched blob cells are.
Blob boundary: [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29], [26, 30], [26, 31], [25, 32], [24, 32]]
The square is at (23-24, 31-32). This is at the top-left of the blob. The blob extends to the right (up to col 37) and down (up to row 31).
To consume more blob cells, I should move the square RIGHT through the blob's interior. The cells to the right of the square (cols 33-37) are likely still blob cells.
Let me try moving right multiple times. Square at (23-24, 31-32). Click TR (23, 33) to move right.
Actually, wait. I already tried moving right once (from 24-25, 30-31 to 23-24, 31-32) and it didn't consume any blob cells. That's because the cells (23, 31), (23, 32), (24, 32) were already outside the blob (or were the boundary).
Hmm, let me reconsider the blob's shape. The boundary goes:
(23,31) → (23,35) → (25,37) → (29,37) → (31,35) → (31,31) → (29,29) → (25,29) → (26,30) → (26,31) → (25,32) → (24,32)
This is a clockwise traversal. The interior is to the right of each edge.
From (23,31) to (23,35): top edge, interior is below (rows > 23)
From (23,35) to (25,37): top-right edge
From (25,37) to (29,37): right edge, interior is to the left (cols < 37)
From (29,37) to (31,35): bottom-right edge
From (31,35) to (31,31): bottom edge, interior is above (rows < 31)
From (31,31) to (29,29): bottom-left edge
From (29,29) to (25,29): left edge, interior is to the right (cols > 29)
From (25,29) to (26,30): ... this is getting complicated
The blob's interior roughly spans rows 24-30, cols 30-36. The square at (23-24, 31-32) is at the top edge. Moving right would go to (23-24, 32-33), which might be inside the blob.
Let me try moving right again and check if blob pixels decrease.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Blob still at 65 pixels. The square only consumed 4 blob cells total when it first entered. The remaining 65 blob cells are untouched. I need to move the square through UNTOUCHED blob cells. The blob spans roughly rows 24-30, cols 30-36. Square is at (23-24, 31-32) at the top edge. Let me try moving RIGHT to go through the blob's interior.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Square at (23-24, 31-32). Move right to go through blob interior
# Click TR (23, 33) to move right
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 23, 'col': 33}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blob = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
if blob:
    print(f"Blob pixels: {blob[0]['pixels']}")
else:
    print("BLOB ELIMINATED!")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['boundary'][0][0] < 30:
        print(f"Square: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
